arXiv.title: Neutrino decays as a natural explanation of the neutrino mass tension
arXiv.comments: 5+4 pages, 7 figures, 1 table. Expanded discussion on goodness-of-fit and particle physics implications. v2 matches the published PRD version. Comments welcome!
arXiv.abstract: A new tension is emerging between the tight cosmological upper bounds on the total neutrino mass ($\sum m_Î½\lesssim 0.06 \, \rm{eV}$) and the lower limits from oscillation experiments, with potentially far-reaching implications for cosmology and particle physics. Neutrinos decaying into massless BSM particles with lifetimes $Ï_Î½\sim 0.01-1\, \rm{Gyr}$ represent a theoretically well-motivated mechanism to reconcile such measurements. Using DESI DR2 and CMB datasets, we show that such decays relax the bound on the total neutrino mass up to $\sum m_Î½< 0.23 \, \rm{eV}$ (95%), restoring full agreement with oscillation data. We also present the first late-time cosmological analysis of neutrino decays into lighter neutrinos in a manner consistent with the measured mass splittings. In contrast to the decays into massless BSM particles, we find that this scenario only marginally alleviates - or even tightens - the cosmological neutrino mass bounds, depending on the mass ordering.
